Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} Year 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}) |Main hall style={{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Annual festival={{ill|lt=April 8|Q2503|WD=Q2503}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''' (かしま あまたらしわけ じんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Watari Town|Q1205296|WD=Q1205296}}, {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is one of the four shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} within {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}} (left hall), {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} (right hall) ** It is said that Takemikazuchi-no-Kami had a child deity named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} (あまたりわけのみこと; there is also a theory that he is the same deity as Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, though this is uncertain{{ill|lt=Toshio Hōga|Q11452691|WD=Q11452691}} “Genealogical Draft,” *The Nakatomi Clan—Descendants of the Ancient Urabe Who Handled Divination: Studies of Ancient Clans (5)*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014). However, according to shrine traditions and historical records, Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto is not enshrined at Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine. ** At {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q17223553|WD=Q17223553}} in Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, and {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}} in Kashima Ward, Minamisōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, there is a tradition that Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto, together with Takemikazuchi-no-Kami and Futsunushi-no-Kami and their child deity Asahiko-no-Mikoto, pacified the Tōhoku region. * In addition, the shrine enshrines deities from nearby shrines: {{ill|lt=Yakumo Shrine|Q11391847|WD=Q11391847}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|UNKNOWN}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}), Kurabanemine Shrine and Takaya Shrine (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}), and {{ill|lt=Kasuga Shrine|Q10855011|WD=Q10855011}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508}}). === History === According to shrine records, on August 6 of the 41st year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}), the shrine was established by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|UNKNOWN}} at Mount Sannmon in the Oforo area of the Jingūji district, north of the present site. On the first day of the fifth month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} ({{ill|lt=782|Q30550|WD=Q30550}}), during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}, prayers were offered, and when the Kashima deity demonstrated divine power in suppressing evil bandits, an imperial decree granted the shrine the rank of Fifth Order and two households. On June 15 of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993}}) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, the shrine was elevated to an official shrine and came to be called “'''Kashima Shrine''',” and on June 18 of the same year it was granted the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255}}), it was relocated to the summit of Kashikiyama in the Kitakashima area of the Kashima district. Thereafter, local lords donated shrine lands, but these holdings were abolished due to the wars between the Date and Sōma clans during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} era (1573–1591). On April 6 of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|WD=Q7664}}), {{ill|lt=Date Saneyoshi|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the fifth head of the {{ill|lt=Watari Date clan|Q11373824|WD=Q11373824}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location. According to the “Watari District Shrine Register,” written in the Meiji period by the chief priest of Kawaguchi Shrine in the Arahama district, Sanbon Kamon no Suke Mononobe no Asomi Masataka, the eldest grandson of '''{{ill|lt=Kagawake|Q17226573|WD=Q17226573|lt=Prince Kagawake}}''', a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, came down to Watari and was appointed as the shrine administrator of the Kashima Three Shrines (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Onata Shrine|Q11677158|WD=Q11677158}}, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine), all listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Anpuku Kawahaku Shrine|Q11451153|WD=Q11451153}}. According to the “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}},” Prince Kagawake is said to have governed {{ill|lt=Uda District|Q11449246|WD=Q11449246}} of Mutsu Province as {{ill|lt=Ukita no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q108487214|WD=Q108487214}}. In November of the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, the shrine administrator post was abolished, and the position of shrine priest (chief priest of prefectural and village shrines) was conferred. The Sanbon family continues to serve as the shrine family to this day. === The Three Kashima Shrines of Watari === In {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, four {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. Among them, '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''', '''Kashima Onata Shrine''' (in the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, Watari Town), and '''Kashima Itsunohike Shrine''' are closely related to {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}}, the Ichinomiya of Hitachi Province in Ibaraki Prefecture. At present, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine still exist. Of these three Kashima shrines, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine are considered two of the “thirty-eight shrines enshrining descendants of the Great Kashima Deity in Mutsu Province” recorded in the “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|UNKNOWN}}.” The small shrine identified as Kashima Itsunohike Shrine was formerly located on privately owned land in Sakuta, Yoshida district, and its shrine building measured three shaku square on all sides. It was deeply revered by the district’s residents, and because its enshrined deity was a war god, many worshippers visited especially during wartime to pray for lasting military fortune and the safety of those going to war. However, as the shrine grounds fell into decline over time, it was merged on February 8 of the 43rd year of Meiji with Tsukiyama Shrine in the same district (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}). File:Haiden of Kashima Onata-jinja.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Onata Shrine (Engishiki-listed shrine) File:Haiden of Tsukiyama-jinja shrine,Watari town..JPG|Haiden of Tsukiyama Shrine At Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, as auxiliary deities, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Itsunohike Shrine, is enshrined in the left hall, and {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Onata Shrine, is enshrined in the right hall. Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, which enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Kami as its principal deity, thus functions as the main shrine of the Three Kashima Shrines of Watari. According to shrine tradition and the head priest, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine enshrines Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine as auxiliary shrines because, at the time Yamato Takeru founded Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine on Mount Sannmon, the three shrines were originally combined into one. Later, during the Heian period, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine was relocated to the Kitakashima area, and during the Edo period it was relocated again to its present site. The other two shrines were also relocated respectively: Kashima Onata Shrine to the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine to Sakuta in the Yoshida district. On the south side of the main hall of Yakumo Shrine, located in the Kitakashima area northeast of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, there stands a stone shrine that dates from the time when the Kashima Shrine was enshrined on Mount Sannmon. This stone shrine is said to have been carried on the backs of devotees from the Jingūji district from Mount Sannmon to Yakumo Shrine. File:Small shrine of Former Kashima-Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone shrine of the Kashima Shrine from the Mount Sannmon period
Enshrined within the precincts of Yakumo Shrine === Grounds === * Shrine buildings (haiden, heiden, honden) File:Kashima-amatarashiwake-jinja in spring.JPG|Springtime grounds of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine as seen from the approach stairs File:Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine * Mikoshiden: A building standing on the right side as one ascends the approach, facing south; it was built in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} ({{ill|lt=1790|Q7775|WD=Q7775}}) by {{ill|lt=Date Murauji|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the 10th head of the Watari Date clan. * Kanameishi (sacred keystone) * Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine * Tō Shrine: The enshrined deity is unknown; according to shrine tradition and the head priest, prayers for recovery were offered here when suffering from smallpox or boils. File:Mikoshiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Mikoshiden (former haiden, built in 1790) File:Kanameishi key-stone of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Kanameishi of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Small shrine of Kashima Onata-jinja and Kashima Itsunohike-jinja shrines.JPG|Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine File:Tou-jinja shrine.JPG|Tō Shrine * In addition, about 1 km east of the shrine precincts, in Fuita, Aikuma Kashima, Watari Town, there is an otabisho (temporary resting place) of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, where the mikoshi procession stops during festivals. File:Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine Office) * “Watari District Shrine Register” (Meiji 32; held by Kawaguchi Shrine) * *Furusato Watari: A Stroll Through History* (Heisei 21; Watari Town) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kashima Amataribetsu Shrine|Q20043641|WD=Q20043641}} === External links === * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010278 Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:宮城県の神社]] [[Category:宮城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:亘理町の歴史]] [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:亘理町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} Year 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}) |Main hall style={{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Annual festival={{ill|lt=April 8|Q2503|WD=Q2503}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''' (かしま あまたらしわけ じんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Watari Town|Q1205296|WD=Q1205296}}, {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is one of the four shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} within {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}} (left hall), {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} (right hall) ** It is said that Takemikazuchi-no-Kami had a child deity named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (あまたりわけのみこと; there is also a theory that he is the same deity as Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, though this is uncertain{{ill|lt=Toshio Hōga|Q11452691|WD=Q11452691}} “Genealogical Draft,” *The Nakatomi Clan—Descendants of the Ancient Urabe Who Handled Divination: Studies of Ancient Clans (5)*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014). However, according to shrine traditions and historical records, Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto is not enshrined at Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine. ** At {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q17223553|WD=Q17223553}} in Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, and {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}} in Kashima Ward, Minamisōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, there is a tradition that Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto, together with Takemikazuchi-no-Kami and Futsunushi-no-Kami and their child deity Asahiko-no-Mikoto, pacified the Tōhoku region. * In addition, the shrine enshrines deities from nearby shrines: {{ill|lt=Yakumo Shrine|Q11391847|WD=Q11391847}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}), Kurabanemine Shrine and Takaya Shrine (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}), and {{ill|lt=Kasuga Shrine|Q10855011|WD=Q10855011}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508}}). === History === According to shrine records, on August 6 of the 41st year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}), the shrine was established by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} at Mount Sannmon in the Oforo area of the Jingūji district, north of the present site. On the first day of the fifth month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} ({{ill|lt=782|Q30550|WD=Q30550}}), during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}, prayers were offered, and when the Kashima deity demonstrated divine power in suppressing evil bandits, an imperial decree granted the shrine the rank of Fifth Order and two households. On June 15 of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993}}) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, the shrine was elevated to an official shrine and came to be called “'''Kashima Shrine''',” and on June 18 of the same year it was granted the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255}}), it was relocated to the summit of Kashikiyama in the Kitakashima area of the Kashima district. Thereafter, local lords donated shrine lands, but these holdings were abolished due to the wars between the Date and Sōma clans during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} era (1573–1591). On April 6 of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|WD=Q7664}}), {{ill|lt=Date Saneyoshi|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the fifth head of the {{ill|lt=Watari Date clan|Q11373824|WD=Q11373824}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location. According to the “Watari District Shrine Register,” written in the Meiji period by the chief priest of Kawaguchi Shrine in the Arahama district, Sanbon Kamon no Suke Mononobe no Asomi Masataka, the eldest grandson of '''{{ill|lt=Kagawake|Q17226573|WD=Q17226573|lt=Prince Kagawake}}''', a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, came down to Watari and was appointed as the shrine administrator of the Kashima Three Shrines (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Onata Shrine|Q11677158|WD=Q11677158}}, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine), all listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Anpuku Kawahaku Shrine|Q11451153|WD=Q11451153}}. According to the “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}},” Prince Kagawake is said to have governed {{ill|lt=Uda District|Q11449246|WD=Q11449246}} of Mutsu Province as {{ill|lt=Ukita no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q108487214|WD=Q108487214}}. In November of the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, the shrine administrator post was abolished, and the position of shrine priest (chief priest of prefectural and village shrines) was conferred. The Sanbon family continues to serve as the shrine family to this day. === The Three Kashima Shrines of Watari === In {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, four {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. Among them, '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''', '''Kashima Onata Shrine''' (in the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, Watari Town), and '''Kashima Itsunohike Shrine''' are closely related to {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}}, the Ichinomiya of Hitachi Province in Ibaraki Prefecture. At present, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine still exist. Of these three Kashima shrines, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine are considered two of the “thirty-eight shrines enshrining descendants of the Great Kashima Deity in Mutsu Province” recorded in the “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The small shrine identified as Kashima Itsunohike Shrine was formerly located on privately owned land in Sakuta, Yoshida district, and its shrine building measured three shaku square on all sides. It was deeply revered by the district’s residents, and because its enshrined deity was a war god, many worshippers visited especially during wartime to pray for lasting military fortune and the safety of those going to war. However, as the shrine grounds fell into decline over time, it was merged on February 8 of the 43rd year of Meiji with Tsukiyama Shrine in the same district (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}). File:Haiden of Kashima Onata-jinja.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Onata Shrine (Engishiki-listed shrine) File:Haiden of Tsukiyama-jinja shrine,Watari town..JPG|Haiden of Tsukiyama Shrine At Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, as auxiliary deities, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Itsunohike Shrine, is enshrined in the left hall, and {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Onata Shrine, is enshrined in the right hall. Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, which enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Kami as its principal deity, thus functions as the main shrine of the Three Kashima Shrines of Watari. According to shrine tradition and the head priest, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine enshrines Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine as auxiliary shrines because, at the time Yamato Takeru founded Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine on Mount Sannmon, the three shrines were originally combined into one. Later, during the Heian period, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine was relocated to the Kitakashima area, and during the Edo period it was relocated again to its present site. The other two shrines were also relocated respectively: Kashima Onata Shrine to the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine to Sakuta in the Yoshida district. On the south side of the main hall of Yakumo Shrine, located in the Kitakashima area northeast of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, there stands a stone shrine that dates from the time when the Kashima Shrine was enshrined on Mount Sannmon. This stone shrine is said to have been carried on the backs of devotees from the Jingūji district from Mount Sannmon to Yakumo Shrine. File:Small shrine of Former Kashima-Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone shrine of the Kashima Shrine from the Mount Sannmon period
Enshrined within the precincts of Yakumo Shrine === Grounds === * Shrine buildings (haiden, heiden, honden) File:Kashima-amatarashiwake-jinja in spring.JPG|Springtime grounds of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine as seen from the approach stairs File:Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine * Mikoshiden: A building standing on the right side as one ascends the approach, facing south; it was built in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} ({{ill|lt=1790|Q7775|WD=Q7775}}) by {{ill|lt=Date Murauji|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the 10th head of the Watari Date clan. * Kanameishi (sacred keystone) * Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine * Tō Shrine: The enshrined deity is unknown; according to shrine tradition and the head priest, prayers for recovery were offered here when suffering from smallpox or boils. File:Mikoshiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Mikoshiden (former haiden, built in 1790) File:Kanameishi key-stone of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Kanameishi of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Small shrine of Kashima Onata-jinja and Kashima Itsunohike-jinja shrines.JPG|Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine File:Tou-jinja shrine.JPG|Tō Shrine * In addition, about 1 km east of the shrine precincts, in Fuita, Aikuma Kashima, Watari Town, there is an otabisho (temporary resting place) of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, where the mikoshi procession stops during festivals. File:Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine Office) * “Watari District Shrine Register” (Meiji 32; held by Kawaguchi Shrine) * *Furusato Watari: A Stroll Through History* (Heisei 21; Watari Town) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kashima Amataribetsu Shrine|Q20043641|WD=Q20043641}} === External links === * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010278 Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:宮城県の神社]] [[Category:宮城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:亘理町の歴史]] [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:亘理町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} Year 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}) |Main hall style={{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Annual festival={{ill|lt=April 8|Q2503|WD=Q2503}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''' (かしま あまたらしわけ じんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Watari Town|Q1205296|WD=Q1205296}}, {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is one of the four shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} within {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}} (left hall), {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} (right hall) ** It is said that Takemikazuchi-no-Kami had a child deity named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (あまたりわけのみこと; there is also a theory that he is the same deity as Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, though this is uncertain{{ill|lt=Toshio Hōga|Q11452691|WD=Q11452691}} “Genealogical Draft,” *The Nakatomi Clan—Descendants of the Ancient Urabe Who Handled Divination: Studies of Ancient Clans (5)*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014). However, according to shrine traditions and historical records, Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto is not enshrined at Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine. ** At {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q17223553|WD=Q17223553}} in Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, and {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}} in Kashima Ward, Minamisōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, there is a tradition that Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto, together with Takemikazuchi-no-Kami and Futsunushi-no-Kami and their child deity Asahiko-no-Mikoto, pacified the Tōhoku region. * In addition, the shrine enshrines deities from nearby shrines: {{ill|lt=Yakumo Shrine|Q11391847|WD=Q11391847}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}), Kurabanemine Shrine and Takaya Shrine (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}), and {{ill|lt=Kasuga Shrine|Q10855011|WD=Q10855011}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508}}). === History === According to shrine records, on August 6 of the 41st year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}), the shrine was established by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} at Mount Sannmon in the Oforo area of the Jingūji district, north of the present site. On the first day of the fifth month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} ({{ill|lt=782|Q30550|WD=Q30550}}), during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}, prayers were offered, and when the Kashima deity demonstrated divine power in suppressing evil bandits, an imperial decree granted the shrine the rank of Fifth Order and two households. On June 15 of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993}}) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, the shrine was elevated to an official shrine and came to be called “'''Kashima Shrine''',” and on June 18 of the same year it was granted the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255}}), it was relocated to the summit of Kashikiyama in the Kitakashima area of the Kashima district. Thereafter, local lords donated shrine lands, but these holdings were abolished due to the wars between the Date and Sōma clans during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} era (1573–1591). On April 6 of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|WD=Q7664}}), {{ill|lt=Date Saneyoshi|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the fifth head of the {{ill|lt=Watari Date clan|Q11373824|WD=Q11373824}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location. According to the “Watari District Shrine Register,” written in the Meiji period by the chief priest of Kawaguchi Shrine in the Arahama district, Sanbon Kamon no Suke Mononobe no Asomi Masataka, the eldest grandson of '''{{ill|lt=Kagawake|Q17226573|WD=Q17226573|lt=Prince Kagawake}}''', a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, came down to Watari and was appointed as the shrine administrator of the Kashima Three Shrines (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Onata Shrine|Q11677158|WD=Q11677158}}, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine), all listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Anpuku Kawahaku Shrine|Q11451153|WD=Q11451153}}. According to the “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}},” Prince Kagawake is said to have governed {{ill|lt=Uda District|Q11449246|WD=Q11449246}} of Mutsu Province as {{ill|lt=Ukita no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q108487214|WD=Q108487214}}. In November of the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, the shrine administrator post was abolished, and the position of shrine priest (chief priest of prefectural and village shrines) was conferred. The Sanbon family continues to serve as the shrine family to this day. === The Three Kashima Shrines of Watari === In {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, four {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} are enshrined. Among them, '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''', '''Kashima Onata Shrine''' (in the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, Watari Town), and '''Kashima Itsunohike Shrine''' are closely related to {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}}, the Ichinomiya of Hitachi Province in Ibaraki Prefecture. At present, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine still exist. Of these three Kashima shrines, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine are considered two of the “thirty-eight shrines enshrining descendants of the Great Kashima Deity in Mutsu Province” recorded in the “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The small shrine identified as Kashima Itsunohike Shrine was formerly located on privately owned land in Sakuta, Yoshida district, and its shrine building measured three shaku square on all sides. It was deeply revered by the district’s residents, and because its enshrined deity was a war god, many worshippers visited especially during wartime to pray for lasting military fortune and the safety of those going to war. However, as the shrine grounds fell into decline over time, it was merged on February 8 of the 43rd year of Meiji with Tsukiyama Shrine in the same district (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}). File:Haiden of Kashima Onata-jinja.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Onata Shrine (Engishiki-listed shrine) File:Haiden of Tsukiyama-jinja shrine,Watari town..JPG|Haiden of Tsukiyama Shrine At Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, as auxiliary deities, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Itsunohike Shrine, is enshrined in the left hall, and {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Onata Shrine, is enshrined in the right hall. Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, which enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Kami as its principal deity, thus functions as the main shrine of the Three Kashima Shrines of Watari. According to shrine tradition and the head priest, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine enshrines Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine as auxiliary shrines because, at the time Yamato Takeru founded Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine on Mount Sannmon, the three shrines were originally combined into one. Later, during the Heian period, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine was relocated to the Kitakashima area, and during the Edo period it was relocated again to its present site. The other two shrines were also relocated respectively: Kashima Onata Shrine to the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine to Sakuta in the Yoshida district. On the south side of the main hall of Yakumo Shrine, located in the Kitakashima area northeast of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, there stands a stone shrine that dates from the time when the Kashima Shrine was enshrined on Mount Sannmon. This stone shrine is said to have been carried on the backs of devotees from the Jingūji district from Mount Sannmon to Yakumo Shrine. File:Small shrine of Former Kashima-Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone shrine of the Kashima Shrine from the Mount Sannmon period
Enshrined within the precincts of Yakumo Shrine === Grounds === * Shrine buildings (haiden, heiden, honden) File:Kashima-amatarashiwake-jinja in spring.JPG|Springtime grounds of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine as seen from the approach stairs File:Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine * Mikoshiden: A building standing on the right side as one ascends the approach, facing south; it was built in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} ({{ill|lt=1790|Q7775|WD=Q7775}}) by {{ill|lt=Date Murauji|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the 10th head of the Watari Date clan. * Kanameishi (sacred keystone) * Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine * Tō Shrine: The enshrined deity is unknown; according to shrine tradition and the head priest, prayers for recovery were offered here when suffering from smallpox or boils. File:Mikoshiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Mikoshiden (former haiden, built in 1790) File:Kanameishi key-stone of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Kanameishi of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Small shrine of Kashima Onata-jinja and Kashima Itsunohike-jinja shrines.JPG|Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine File:Tou-jinja shrine.JPG|Tō Shrine * In addition, about 1 km east of the shrine precincts, in Fuita, Aikuma Kashima, Watari Town, there is an otabisho (temporary resting place) of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, where the mikoshi procession stops during festivals. File:Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine Office) * “Watari District Shrine Register” (Meiji 32; held by Kawaguchi Shrine) * *Furusato Watari: A Stroll Through History* (Heisei 21; Watari Town) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kashima Amataribetsu Shrine|Q20043641|WD=Q20043641}} === External links === * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010278 Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:宮城県の神社]] [[Category:宮城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:亘理町の歴史]] [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:亘理町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} Year 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}) |Main hall style={{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Annual festival={{ill|lt=April 8|Q2503|WD=Q2503}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''' (かしま あまたらしわけ じんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Watari Town|Q1205296|WD=Q1205296}}, {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is one of the four shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} within {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}} (left hall), {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} (right hall) ** It is said that Takemikazuchi-no-Kami had a child deity named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (あまたりわけのみこと; there is also a theory that he is the same deity as Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, though this is uncertain{{ill|lt=Toshio Hōga|Q11452691|WD=Q11452691}} “Genealogical Draft,” *The Nakatomi Clan—Descendants of the Ancient Urabe Who Handled Divination: Studies of Ancient Clans (5)*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014). However, according to shrine traditions and historical records, Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto is not enshrined at Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine. ** At {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q17223553|WD=Q17223553}} in Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, and {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}} in Kashima Ward, Minamisōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, there is a tradition that Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto, together with Takemikazuchi-no-Kami and Futsunushi-no-Kami and their child deity Asahiko-no-Mikoto, pacified the Tōhoku region. * In addition, the shrine enshrines deities from nearby shrines: {{ill|lt=Yakumo Shrine|Q11391847|WD=Q11391847}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q11390939}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}), Kurabanemine Shrine and Takaya Shrine (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}), and {{ill|lt=Kasuga Shrine|Q10855011|WD=Q10855011}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508}}). === History === According to shrine records, on August 6 of the 41st year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}), the shrine was established by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} at Mount Sannmon in the Oforo area of the Jingūji district, north of the present site. On the first day of the fifth month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} ({{ill|lt=782|Q30550|WD=Q30550}}), during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}, prayers were offered, and when the Kashima deity demonstrated divine power in suppressing evil bandits, an imperial decree granted the shrine the rank of Fifth Order and two households. On June 15 of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993}}) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, the shrine was elevated to an official shrine and came to be called “'''Kashima Shrine''',” and on June 18 of the same year it was granted the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255}}), it was relocated to the summit of Kashikiyama in the Kitakashima area of the Kashima district. Thereafter, local lords donated shrine lands, but these holdings were abolished due to the wars between the Date and Sōma clans during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} era (1573–1591). On April 6 of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|WD=Q7664}}), {{ill|lt=Date Saneyoshi|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the fifth head of the {{ill|lt=Watari Date clan|Q11373824|WD=Q11373824}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location. According to the “Watari District Shrine Register,” written in the Meiji period by the chief priest of Kawaguchi Shrine in the Arahama district, Sanbon Kamon no Suke Mononobe no Asomi Masataka, the eldest grandson of '''{{ill|lt=Kagawake|Q17226573|WD=Q17226573|lt=Prince Kagawake}}''', a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, came down to Watari and was appointed as the shrine administrator of the Kashima Three Shrines (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Onata Shrine|Q11677158|WD=Q11677158}}, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine), all listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Anpuku Kawahaku Shrine|Q11451153|WD=Q11451153}}. According to the “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}},” Prince Kagawake is said to have governed {{ill|lt=Uda District|Q11449246|WD=Q11449246}} of Mutsu Province as {{ill|lt=Ukita no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q108487214|WD=Q108487214}}. In November of the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, the shrine administrator post was abolished, and the position of shrine priest (chief priest of prefectural and village shrines) was conferred. The Sanbon family continues to serve as the shrine family to this day. === The Three Kashima Shrines of Watari === In {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, four {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} are enshrined. Among them, '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''', '''Kashima Onata Shrine''' (in the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, Watari Town), and '''Kashima Itsunohike Shrine''' are closely related to {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}}, the Ichinomiya of Hitachi Province in Ibaraki Prefecture. At present, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine still exist. Of these three Kashima shrines, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine are considered two of the “thirty-eight shrines enshrining descendants of the Great Kashima Deity in Mutsu Province” recorded in the “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The small shrine identified as Kashima Itsunohike Shrine was formerly located on privately owned land in Sakuta, Yoshida district, and its shrine building measured three shaku square on all sides. It was deeply revered by the district’s residents, and because its enshrined deity was a war god, many worshippers visited especially during wartime to pray for lasting military fortune and the safety of those going to war. However, as the shrine grounds fell into decline over time, it was merged on February 8 of the 43rd year of Meiji with Tsukiyama Shrine in the same district (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}). File:Haiden of Kashima Onata-jinja.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Onata Shrine (Engishiki-listed shrine) File:Haiden of Tsukiyama-jinja shrine,Watari town..JPG|Haiden of Tsukiyama Shrine At Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, as auxiliary deities, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Itsunohike Shrine, is enshrined in the left hall, and {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Onata Shrine, is enshrined in the right hall. Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, which enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Kami as its principal deity, thus functions as the main shrine of the Three Kashima Shrines of Watari. According to shrine tradition and the head priest, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine enshrines Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine as auxiliary shrines because, at the time Yamato Takeru founded Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine on Mount Sannmon, the three shrines were originally combined into one. Later, during the Heian period, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine was relocated to the Kitakashima area, and during the Edo period it was relocated again to its present site. The other two shrines were also relocated respectively: Kashima Onata Shrine to the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine to Sakuta in the Yoshida district. On the south side of the main hall of Yakumo Shrine, located in the Kitakashima area northeast of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, there stands a stone shrine that dates from the time when the Kashima Shrine was enshrined on Mount Sannmon. This stone shrine is said to have been carried on the backs of devotees from the Jingūji district from Mount Sannmon to Yakumo Shrine. File:Small shrine of Former Kashima-Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone shrine of the Kashima Shrine from the Mount Sannmon period
Enshrined within the precincts of Yakumo Shrine === Grounds === * Shrine buildings (haiden, heiden, honden) File:Kashima-amatarashiwake-jinja in spring.JPG|Springtime grounds of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine as seen from the approach stairs File:Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine * Mikoshiden: A building standing on the right side as one ascends the approach, facing south; it was built in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} ({{ill|lt=1790|Q7775|WD=Q7775}}) by {{ill|lt=Date Murauji|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the 10th head of the Watari Date clan. * Kanameishi (sacred keystone) * Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine * Tō Shrine: The enshrined deity is unknown; according to shrine tradition and the head priest, prayers for recovery were offered here when suffering from smallpox or boils. File:Mikoshiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Mikoshiden (former haiden, built in 1790) File:Kanameishi key-stone of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Kanameishi of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Small shrine of Kashima Onata-jinja and Kashima Itsunohike-jinja shrines.JPG|Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine File:Tou-jinja shrine.JPG|Tō Shrine * In addition, about 1 km east of the shrine precincts, in Fuita, Aikuma Kashima, Watari Town, there is an otabisho (temporary resting place) of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, where the mikoshi procession stops during festivals. File:Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine Office) * “Watari District Shrine Register” (Meiji 32; held by Kawaguchi Shrine) * *Furusato Watari: A Stroll Through History* (Heisei 21; Watari Town) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kashima Amataribetsu Shrine|Q20043641|WD=Q20043641}} === External links === * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010278 Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:宮城県の神社]] [[Category:宮城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:亘理町の歴史]] [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:亘理町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} Year 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}) |Main hall style={{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Annual festival={{ill|lt=April 8|Q2503|WD=Q2503}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''' (かしま あまたらしわけ じんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Watari Town|Q1205296|WD=Q1205296}}, {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is one of the four shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} within {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}} (left hall), {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} (right hall) ** It is said that Takemikazuchi-no-Kami had a child deity named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (あまたりわけのみこと; there is also a theory that he is the same deity as Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, though this is uncertain{{ill|lt=Toshio Hōga|Q11452691|WD=Q11452691}} “Genealogical Draft,” *The Nakatomi Clan—Descendants of the Ancient Urabe Who Handled Divination: Studies of Ancient Clans (5)*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014). However, according to shrine traditions and historical records, Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto is not enshrined at Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine. ** At {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q17223553|WD=Q17223553}} in Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, and {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}} in Kashima Ward, Minamisōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, there is a tradition that Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto, together with Takemikazuchi-no-Kami and Futsunushi-no-Kami and their child deity Asahiko-no-Mikoto, pacified the Tōhoku region. * In addition, the shrine enshrines deities from nearby shrines: {{ill|lt=Yakumo Shrine|Q11391847|WD=Q11391847}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q11390939}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}), Kurabanemine Shrine and Takaya Shrine (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}), and {{ill|lt=Kasuga Shrine|Q10855011|WD=Q10855011}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508}}). === History === According to shrine records, on August 6 of the 41st year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}), the shrine was established by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q461258}} at Mount Sannmon in the Oforo area of the Jingūji district, north of the present site. On the first day of the fifth month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} ({{ill|lt=782|Q30550|WD=Q30550}}), during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}, prayers were offered, and when the Kashima deity demonstrated divine power in suppressing evil bandits, an imperial decree granted the shrine the rank of Fifth Order and two households. On June 15 of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993}}) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, the shrine was elevated to an official shrine and came to be called “'''Kashima Shrine''',” and on June 18 of the same year it was granted the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255}}), it was relocated to the summit of Kashikiyama in the Kitakashima area of the Kashima district. Thereafter, local lords donated shrine lands, but these holdings were abolished due to the wars between the Date and Sōma clans during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} era (1573–1591). On April 6 of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|WD=Q7664}}), {{ill|lt=Date Saneyoshi|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the fifth head of the {{ill|lt=Watari Date clan|Q11373824|WD=Q11373824}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location. According to the “Watari District Shrine Register,” written in the Meiji period by the chief priest of Kawaguchi Shrine in the Arahama district, Sanbon Kamon no Suke Mononobe no Asomi Masataka, the eldest grandson of '''{{ill|lt=Kagawake|Q17226573|WD=Q17226573|lt=Prince Kagawake}}''', a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, came down to Watari and was appointed as the shrine administrator of the Kashima Three Shrines (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Onata Shrine|Q11677158|WD=Q11677158}}, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine), all listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Anpuku Kawahaku Shrine|Q11451153|WD=Q11451153}}. According to the “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}},” Prince Kagawake is said to have governed {{ill|lt=Uda District|Q11449246|WD=Q11449246}} of Mutsu Province as {{ill|lt=Ukita no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q108487214|WD=Q108487214}}. In November of the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, the shrine administrator post was abolished, and the position of shrine priest (chief priest of prefectural and village shrines) was conferred. The Sanbon family continues to serve as the shrine family to this day. === The Three Kashima Shrines of Watari === In {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, four {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} are enshrined. Among them, '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''', '''Kashima Onata Shrine''' (in the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, Watari Town), and '''Kashima Itsunohike Shrine''' are closely related to {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}}, the Ichinomiya of Hitachi Province in Ibaraki Prefecture. At present, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine still exist. Of these three Kashima shrines, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine are considered two of the “thirty-eight shrines enshrining descendants of the Great Kashima Deity in Mutsu Province” recorded in the “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The small shrine identified as Kashima Itsunohike Shrine was formerly located on privately owned land in Sakuta, Yoshida district, and its shrine building measured three shaku square on all sides. It was deeply revered by the district’s residents, and because its enshrined deity was a war god, many worshippers visited especially during wartime to pray for lasting military fortune and the safety of those going to war. However, as the shrine grounds fell into decline over time, it was merged on February 8 of the 43rd year of Meiji with Tsukiyama Shrine in the same district (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}). File:Haiden of Kashima Onata-jinja.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Onata Shrine (Engishiki-listed shrine) File:Haiden of Tsukiyama-jinja shrine,Watari town..JPG|Haiden of Tsukiyama Shrine At Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, as auxiliary deities, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Itsunohike Shrine, is enshrined in the left hall, and {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Onata Shrine, is enshrined in the right hall. Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, which enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Kami as its principal deity, thus functions as the main shrine of the Three Kashima Shrines of Watari. According to shrine tradition and the head priest, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine enshrines Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine as auxiliary shrines because, at the time Yamato Takeru founded Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine on Mount Sannmon, the three shrines were originally combined into one. Later, during the Heian period, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine was relocated to the Kitakashima area, and during the Edo period it was relocated again to its present site. The other two shrines were also relocated respectively: Kashima Onata Shrine to the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine to Sakuta in the Yoshida district. On the south side of the main hall of Yakumo Shrine, located in the Kitakashima area northeast of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, there stands a stone shrine that dates from the time when the Kashima Shrine was enshrined on Mount Sannmon. This stone shrine is said to have been carried on the backs of devotees from the Jingūji district from Mount Sannmon to Yakumo Shrine. File:Small shrine of Former Kashima-Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone shrine of the Kashima Shrine from the Mount Sannmon period
Enshrined within the precincts of Yakumo Shrine === Grounds === * Shrine buildings (haiden, heiden, honden) File:Kashima-amatarashiwake-jinja in spring.JPG|Springtime grounds of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine as seen from the approach stairs File:Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine * Mikoshiden: A building standing on the right side as one ascends the approach, facing south; it was built in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} ({{ill|lt=1790|Q7775|WD=Q7775}}) by {{ill|lt=Date Murauji|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the 10th head of the Watari Date clan. * Kanameishi (sacred keystone) * Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine * Tō Shrine: The enshrined deity is unknown; according to shrine tradition and the head priest, prayers for recovery were offered here when suffering from smallpox or boils. File:Mikoshiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Mikoshiden (former haiden, built in 1790) File:Kanameishi key-stone of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Kanameishi of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Small shrine of Kashima Onata-jinja and Kashima Itsunohike-jinja shrines.JPG|Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine File:Tou-jinja shrine.JPG|Tō Shrine * In addition, about 1 km east of the shrine precincts, in Fuita, Aikuma Kashima, Watari Town, there is an otabisho (temporary resting place) of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, where the mikoshi procession stops during festivals. File:Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine Office) * “Watari District Shrine Register” (Meiji 32; held by Kawaguchi Shrine) * *Furusato Watari: A Stroll Through History* (Heisei 21; Watari Town) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kashima Amataribetsu Shrine|Q20043641|WD=Q20043641}} === External links === * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010278 Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:宮城県の神社]] [[Category:宮城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:亘理町の歴史]] [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:亘理町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} Year 41 ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}) |Main hall style={{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Annual festival={{ill|lt=April 8|Q2503|WD=Q2503}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''' (かしま あまたらしわけ じんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Watari Town|Q1205296|WD=Q1205296}}, {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is one of the four shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} within {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}} (left hall), {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} (right hall) ** It is said that Takemikazuchi-no-Kami had a child deity named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (あまたりわけのみこと; there is also a theory that he is the same deity as Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, though this is uncertain{{ill|lt=Toshio Hōga|Q11452691|WD=Q11452691}} “Genealogical Draft,” *The Nakatomi Clan—Descendants of the Ancient Urabe Who Handled Divination: Studies of Ancient Clans (5)*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014). However, according to shrine traditions and historical records, Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto is not enshrined at Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine. ** At {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q17223553|WD=Q17223553}} in Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, and {{ill|lt=Kashima Miko Shrine|Q11677124|WD=Q11677124}} in Kashima Ward, Minamisōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, there is a tradition that Amataribetsu-no-Mikoto, together with Takemikazuchi-no-Kami and Futsunushi-no-Kami and their child deity Asahiko-no-Mikoto, pacified the Tōhoku region. * In addition, the shrine enshrines deities from nearby shrines: {{ill|lt=Yakumo Shrine|Q11391847|WD=Q11391847}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q11390939}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}), Kurabanemine Shrine and Takaya Shrine (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi-no-Kami|Q2842779|WD=Q2842779}}), and {{ill|lt=Kasuga Shrine|Q10855011|WD=Q10855011}} (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508}}). === History === According to shrine records, on August 6 of the 41st year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} ({{ill|lt=111|Q30640|WD=Q30640}}), the shrine was established by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q461258}} at Mount Sannmon in the Oforo area of the Jingūji district, north of the present site. On the first day of the fifth month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} ({{ill|lt=782|Q30550|WD=Q30550}}), during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kanmu|Q314846|WD=Q314846}}, prayers were offered, and when the Kashima deity demonstrated divine power in suppressing evil bandits, an imperial decree granted the shrine the rank of Fifth Order and two households. On June 15 of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=862|Q24993|WD=Q24993}}) during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}, the shrine was elevated to an official shrine and came to be called “'''Kashima Shrine''',” and on June 18 of the same year it was granted the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} ({{ill|lt=941|Q24255|WD=Q24255}}), it was relocated to the summit of Kashikiyama in the Kitakashima area of the Kashima district. Thereafter, local lords donated shrine lands, but these holdings were abolished due to the wars between the Date and Sōma clans during the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} era (1573–1591). On April 6 of the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|WD=Q7664}}), {{ill|lt=Date Saneyoshi|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the fifth head of the {{ill|lt=Watari Date clan|Q11373824|WD=Q11373824}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location. According to the “Watari District Shrine Register,” written in the Meiji period by the chief priest of Kawaguchi Shrine in the Arahama district, Sanbon Kamon no Suke Mononobe no Asomi Masataka, the eldest grandson of '''{{ill|lt=Kagawake|Q17226573|WD=Q17226573|lt=Prince Kagawake}}''', a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, came down to Watari and was appointed as the shrine administrator of the Kashima Three Shrines (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kashima Onata Shrine|Q11677158|WD=Q11677158}}, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine), all listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Anpuku Kawahaku Shrine|Q11451153|WD=Q11451153}}. According to the “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}},” Prince Kagawake is said to have governed {{ill|lt=Uda District|Q11449246|WD=Q11449246}} of Mutsu Province as {{ill|lt=Ukita no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q108487214|WD=Q108487214}}. In November of the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, the shrine administrator post was abolished, and the position of shrine priest (chief priest of prefectural and village shrines) was conferred. The Sanbon family continues to serve as the shrine family to this day. === The Three Kashima Shrines of Watari === In {{ill|lt=Watari District|Q1131765|WD=Q1131765}}, four {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} are enshrined. Among them, '''Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine''', '''Kashima Onata Shrine''' (in the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, Watari Town), and '''Kashima Itsunohike Shrine''' are closely related to {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}}, the Ichinomiya of Hitachi Province in Ibaraki Prefecture. At present, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine still exist. Of these three Kashima shrines, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine and Kashima Onata Shrine are considered two of the “thirty-eight shrines enshrining descendants of the Great Kashima Deity in Mutsu Province” recorded in the “{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1052981|lt=Sandai Jitsuroku|1=UNKNOWN}}.” The small shrine identified as Kashima Itsunohike Shrine was formerly located on privately owned land in Sakuta, Yoshida district, and its shrine building measured three shaku square on all sides. It was deeply revered by the district’s residents, and because its enshrined deity was a war god, many worshippers visited especially during wartime to pray for lasting military fortune and the safety of those going to war. However, as the shrine grounds fell into decline over time, it was merged on February 8 of the 43rd year of Meiji with Tsukiyama Shrine in the same district (enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}). File:Haiden of Kashima Onata-jinja.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Onata Shrine (Engishiki-listed shrine) File:Haiden of Tsukiyama-jinja shrine,Watari town..JPG|Haiden of Tsukiyama Shrine At Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, as auxiliary deities, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Ohabari|Q10939046|WD=Q10939046|lt=Itsunoohabari-no-Kami}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Itsunohike Shrine, is enshrined in the left hall, and {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}}, said to be the enshrined deity of Kashima Onata Shrine, is enshrined in the right hall. Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, which enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Kami as its principal deity, thus functions as the main shrine of the Three Kashima Shrines of Watari. According to shrine tradition and the head priest, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine enshrines Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine as auxiliary shrines because, at the time Yamato Takeru founded Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine on Mount Sannmon, the three shrines were originally combined into one. Later, during the Heian period, Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine was relocated to the Kitakashima area, and during the Edo period it was relocated again to its present site. The other two shrines were also relocated respectively: Kashima Onata Shrine to the Koyama area of the Aikuma district, and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine to Sakuta in the Yoshida district. On the south side of the main hall of Yakumo Shrine, located in the Kitakashima area northeast of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, there stands a stone shrine that dates from the time when the Kashima Shrine was enshrined on Mount Sannmon. This stone shrine is said to have been carried on the backs of devotees from the Jingūji district from Mount Sannmon to Yakumo Shrine. File:Small shrine of Former Kashima-Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Stone shrine of the Kashima Shrine from the Mount Sannmon period
Enshrined within the precincts of Yakumo Shrine === Grounds === * Shrine buildings (haiden, heiden, honden) File:Kashima-amatarashiwake-jinja in spring.JPG|Springtime grounds of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Torii gate of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Haiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine as seen from the approach stairs File:Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Honden of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine * Mikoshiden: A building standing on the right side as one ascends the approach, facing south; it was built in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} ({{ill|lt=1790|Q7775|WD=Q7775}}) by {{ill|lt=Date Murauji|Q11380755|WD=Q11380755}}, the 10th head of the Watari Date clan. * Kanameishi (sacred keystone) * Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine * Tō Shrine: The enshrined deity is unknown; according to shrine tradition and the head priest, prayers for recovery were offered here when suffering from smallpox or boils. File:Mikoshiden of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Mikoshiden (former haiden, built in 1790) File:Kanameishi key-stone of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Kanameishi of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine File:Small shrine of Kashima Onata-jinja and Kashima Itsunohike-jinja shrines.JPG|Stone shrines of Kashima Onata Shrine and Kashima Itsunohike Shrine File:Tou-jinja shrine.JPG|Tō Shrine * In addition, about 1 km east of the shrine precincts, in Fuita, Aikuma Kashima, Watari Town, there is an otabisho (temporary resting place) of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine, where the mikoshi procession stops during festivals. File:Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake-jinja shrine.JPG|Otabisho of Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine === Notes === {{Help for notes}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine (Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine Office) * “Watari District Shrine Register” (Meiji 32; held by Kawaguchi Shrine) * *Furusato Watari: A Stroll Through History* (Heisei 21; Watari Town) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kashima Amataribetsu Shrine|Q20043641|WD=Q20043641}} === External links === * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010278 Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Amatarashiwake Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kashima Amatarashi Wake Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Subshrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:宮城県の神社]] [[Category:宮城県の旧郷社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:亘理町の歴史]] [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:亘理町の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]
